title: The Landing Page is Live
excerpt: Playground's new landing page — ideated, designed, and coded by me — is done. Three weeks from concept to a fully built site, shipped alongside everything else.

sections.activities:
The biggest news: Playground's new landing page website is complete. I ideated the concept, designed every screen, and coded the entire thing myself — HTML, CSS, JavaScript, responsive layouts, animations, all of it. From the first sketch to a fully working, production-ready site in about three weeks, all while keeping my other projects moving forward. It's easily the most ambitious thing I've done during this internship.

The process was intense. It started with rethinking the narrative — what story should the landing page tell, what should a visitor feel within the first few seconds, how do we communicate Playground's value without overwhelming people. Then I moved into high-fidelity design, got feedback from the CEO and team, iterated, and jumped straight into code. Being able to design and build it end-to-end meant there was no handoff gap — every design decision translated directly into the implementation exactly as intended.

In parallel, I wrapped up final refinements on the background remover and image converter based on post-launch data. We noticed some drop-off in the conversion flow, so I redesigned the confirmation step to be clearer. Small change, but the numbers improved within days.

I also presented a creator dashboard concept I've been developing to the full leadership team — CEO, COO, and engineering leads. It was a 20-minute presentation followed by Q&A. Nerve-wracking but it went well.
sections.deadlines:
The landing page being done is the biggest milestone of the internship. Delivering a project of that scope — idea to coded site — while managing other active workstreams took serious time management.

Completed:

  • ✓ Playground landing page — ideated, designed, and coded
  • ✓ Creator dashboard — concept presented to leadership
  • ✓ BG remover conversion flow optimization

Next up: Engineering handoff for the creator dashboard, final landing page polish and performance tuning, and starting to document my design decisions and processes so the team can continue iterating after my internship ends.
sections.learning:
The landing page project crystallized something important: being able to both design and code is a superpower. When you can go from idea to implementation without depending on someone else to translate your vision, the result is more cohesive and the process is dramatically faster. Design decisions that would normally require a back-and-forth with a developer — "can we do this animation?", "is this layout feasible?" — I could just try and find out immediately.

I also learned a lot about managing energy across parallel projects. There were days where I'd spend the morning on post-launch fixes for the creator tools, the afternoon coding the landing page, and the evening refining the creator dashboard concept. It required being very intentional about context-switching and knowing when to go deep vs. when to stay shallow.

Something less tangible but important: I've started to understand when to push for my ideas and when to let go. Not every hill is worth dying on, and sometimes a good-enough solution shipped today is better than a perfect one shipped next month.
sections.environment:
I feel fully integrated into the team now. People come to me with design questions, not just about my projects but about general product direction. That shift from "the intern" to "a designer on the team" has been gradual but meaningful.

Showing the finished landing page to the team was a great moment. A few people were genuinely surprised that I'd designed and built it — I think there's still an assumption that designers don't code, or coders don't design. Breaking that expectation felt good.

I've also been mentoring a newer team member on some design system questions, which is a funny reversal from where I started just a few months ago.
sections.strengths:
Full-stack creative ownership — taking a project from ideation through design to coded implementation without needing a handoff.

Parallel execution — shipping the landing page while maintaining quality on the creator tools and dashboard work.

Communicating design rationale to non-design stakeholders in a way that connects to business outcomes.
sections.growth:
Time management when juggling multiple projects at different stages — I sometimes spend too long polishing one thing while another needs attention.

Delegating or saying no — I've been taking on a lot and need to be more realistic about capacity.
sections.growthPlan:
I've started time-boxing my work more strictly — allocating specific hours to each project rather than letting one consume the whole day.

I'm also practicing being upfront about timelines when new requests come in, rather than just saying yes and figuring it out later.
sections.positive:
If I had to pick one moment that defines this internship, it might be sitting at my desk at the end of a long day, refreshing the browser, and seeing the finished Playground landing page — something that three weeks earlier was just a rough idea in my head. I ideated it, designed it, and coded every line of it. That full loop from concept to creation is exactly the kind of work I want to do.

But honestly, the thing that makes me proudest is looking back at where I started. Twelve weeks ago I was nervous about speaking up in meetings. Now I'm leading presentations, proposing product strategy, and building entire websites from scratch. The growth has been real, and I owe a lot of it to this team for giving me space to figure things out.

title: Shipping and Building
excerpt: The background remover went live, I'm knee-deep in coding Playground's new landing page, and learning what it means to juggle a live product with a major build at the same time.

sections.activities:
The background remover officially launched this period. Seeing something I designed go live to real users — tens of thousands of creators — is a feeling I wasn't fully prepared for. It's exciting but also a bit terrifying knowing that real people are interacting with my work every day.

Post-launch, I've been closely monitoring usage data and user feedback. We set up analytics on the key flows and I've been tracking completion rates, error states, and where users drop off. This has already surfaced a few areas where the UX could be smoother.

The image converter is in final QA and should launch next week. I've been working with the engineering team to resolve some edge cases around file format support and making sure the error messages are helpful rather than cryptic.

But the bulk of my energy has been going into the Playground landing page redesign. The designs are locked in after a few rounds of feedback with leadership, and I've moved into the coding phase. I'm building it out myself — HTML, CSS, JavaScript, responsive behavior, animations, the whole thing. It's been intense doing this alongside the creator tools work, but there's something really satisfying about going from a blank Figma artboard to a working, interactive website. The design-to-code muscle is one I've wanted to develop, and this project is the perfect exercise for it.
sections.deadlines:
Biggest milestone: background remover shipped on schedule. That felt good after weeks of iteration.

Status update:

  • ✓ Background remover — live
  • ✓ Post-launch monitoring and quick fixes
  • → Image converter — in QA, launching next week
  • → Playground landing page — designs finalized, actively coding

Coming up: Image converter launch support, finishing the landing page build, and polishing responsive behavior and performance. Aiming to have the landing page coded out within the next couple of weeks.
sections.learning:
The biggest lesson these two weeks: designing a feature is only half the job. Supporting the background remover after launch — watching how people actually use it, responding to feedback, fixing issues — is a whole other skill set.

On the landing page, coding my own designs has been incredibly educational. There are things I designed in Figma that looked great but were tricky to implement well — complex responsive layouts, smooth scroll animations, performance-conscious image loading. Having to solve those problems myself makes me a much more thoughtful designer. I now instinctively think about how something will be built while I'm still designing it.

I also had a useful conversation with my mentor about design maturity — the idea that early in your career you focus on making things look good, then you learn to make things work well, and eventually you learn to make the right things. Bridging design and code feels like it accelerates that journey.
sections.environment:
The post-launch energy in the office has been great. There's something about seeing real usage numbers tick up that brings the team together. We had an informal celebration when the background remover hit its first-day usage target.

Working on the landing page has been a more solo endeavor compared to the collaborative feature work. I'm heads-down coding for long stretches, then sharing progress with the team for feedback. It's a different rhythm — more focused and independent — and I'm enjoying it. A few engineers have been impressed that I'm doing the implementation myself, which feels good.

Atlanta is starting to feel like home. I've found my regular coffee shop, figured out the best lunch spots near the office, and even joined a weekend basketball pickup game with some of the engineers.
sections.strengths:
Ability to work across design and code — going from concept to implementation without a handoff gap.

Managing parallel workstreams effectively — shipping creator tools while building the landing page simultaneously.

Building strong working relationships across different teams (design, engineering, data).
sections.growth:
Being more proactive about documenting design decisions — I tend to keep rationale in my head rather than writing it down, which makes it harder for others to understand or build on my work.

Knowing when to stop polishing code and ship — I can get caught in perfecting animations and micro-interactions when the bigger picture is already solid.
sections.growthPlan:
I've started keeping a simple decision log for each project — noting what I chose, why, and what alternatives I considered. It takes five minutes but saves hours of re-explaining later.

For the landing page, I've set a hard deadline for myself and a checklist of must-haves vs. nice-to-haves. If it's not on the must-have list, it waits until after launch.
sections.positive:
Watching real creators use the background remover and seeing positive feedback on social media has been surreal. One creator posted a video saying it saved them "hours of work" — that's the kind of impact I got into design for.

And honestly, sitting in the office late with headphones on, coding the landing page and watching it come to life section by section — that's been one of my favorite parts of this entire internship. There's a flow state you get into when you're building something you designed yourself, and it's unlike anything else. Can't wait to share the finished product.

title: From Design to Development
excerpt: Handing off creator tool designs to engineering, shipping the Alice landing page, and kicking off a big new project — redesigning Playground's main landing page from scratch.

sections.activities:
These two weeks have been all about the transition from design to development. The background remover and image converter designs are finalized, and I've been working closely with the engineering team to make sure everything translates properly from Figma to code.

This involved creating detailed design specifications — documenting spacing, interactions, edge cases, error states, empty states, and responsive behavior. It's the kind of work that isn't glamorous but makes a huge difference in whether the final product matches the design intent.

The Alice landing page launched earlier this period. Seeing it live was a milestone — my first shipped project at Playground. The team was happy with how it turned out, and we're already seeing good engagement metrics from the page.

But the biggest development this period is a new project that's quickly become my main focus: redesigning Playground's main landing page website. The current site needed a refresh, and after some discussions with leadership I pitched a new direction. The response was really positive, so now I'm leading the whole thing — from concept to final implementation. The plan is to not just design it but also code it out myself, which is exciting and a little intimidating. These first two weeks have been deep in the ideation phase: studying competitor sites, defining the narrative structure, exploring visual directions, and sketching out the information architecture.

I've also been running usability tests on the background remover flow with a small group of creators. The feedback has been really valuable and led to a few meaningful iterations before the feature ships.
sections.deadlines:
The Alice landing page shipping was a big checkpoint. Feels good to have that one out the door.

Status update:

  • ✓ Alice landing page — shipped and live
  • ✓ Background remover — design specs complete, in development
  • ✓ Image converter — design specs complete, development starting
  • → Playground landing page — ideation and early concepts underway
  • ✓ Usability testing round 1 — completed, iterations made

Coming up: Moving the landing page redesign into high-fidelity design, supporting the engineering team through creator tools implementation, and QA reviews on the background remover build.
sections.learning:
The biggest learning has been around design handoff. I thought having clean Figma files would be enough, but there's so much context that doesn't live in the mockups — why certain decisions were made, how things should behave at different screen sizes, what happens in error states. Writing good specs is a real skill.

Starting the landing page project has also been a lesson in thinking about design at the brand level. A product landing page isn't just UI — it's storytelling. How do you communicate what Playground is and why someone should care, all within a few seconds of scrolling? That's a very different design challenge than feature work.

I've also been learning about technical constraints more deeply. Working with engineers on the creator tools, I now understand why certain animations might cause performance issues, or why a design that looks simple might be complex to implement. That knowledge is already feeding into how I'm thinking about the landing page — I want to design something I can actually build well.
sections.environment:
My relationship with the engineering team has grown a lot. Sitting near them in the office means I can quickly answer questions when they're implementing my designs, and they teach me things about how the product works under the hood.

There's a healthy tension between design and engineering that I'm learning to navigate. Sometimes I push for a detail that matters for the user experience, and sometimes I accept a simpler implementation that gets us to launch faster. Finding that balance is an ongoing negotiation.

Getting the green light on the landing page project from leadership felt like a big vote of confidence. My mentor pointed out that I've become noticeably more confident in meetings, which was nice to hear. I think it's a combination of knowing the product better, having shipped something, and just being more comfortable with the team.
sections.strengths:
Creating thorough design specifications that reduce back-and-forth during development.

Taking initiative on the landing page project — identifying the opportunity and pitching the idea rather than waiting for it to be assigned.

Adapting quickly when user testing reveals issues — not getting attached to initial solutions.
sections.growth:
Scoping a large project realistically — the landing page is ambitious and I need to make sure I can deliver it alongside my other work.

Being more decisive when there are multiple valid approaches — I sometimes spend too long evaluating options when either would work fine.
sections.growthPlan:
For the landing page scope, I've created a rough timeline breaking it into phases: ideation (done), design, code, and polish. Having those checkpoints helps me stay honest about progress.

For decisiveness, I'm giving myself time limits for decisions that don't have high stakes. If two options are roughly equal, pick one and move forward. Iteration will reveal the right answer faster than deliberation.
sections.positive:
The Alice landing page going live was a genuinely emotional moment. I took a screenshot of it on my phone and sent it to my family. It's one thing to design something in Figma — it's another to see it on a real website that real people visit.

And then getting the go-ahead on the Playground landing page redesign on top of that — it feels like the team trusts me with increasingly important work. Going from designing a feature component to owning the company's front door to the world is a pretty big leap, and I'm determined to make it count.

title: Deep in the Details
excerpt: Finalizing the Alice landing page, diving deeper into feature design for creator tools, and running my first usability sessions with real users.

sections.activities:
These two weeks were some of the most heads-down design work so far. The Alice landing page is nearly complete — we went through several rounds of feedback with leadership and iterated on the messaging, visual hierarchy, and call-to-action placement. It's been a great exercise in refining a design through multiple review cycles.

On the creator tools side, I've been working on high-fidelity designs for the background remover. This means moving beyond wireframes into fully realized UI — colors, typography, spacing, interaction states, all within Playground's design system. Getting the details right at this stage matters a lot because these are the files that engineers will actually build from.

I also started designing the image converter flow in more detail. The challenge here is keeping it simple when there are many possible input and output formats. I've been exploring different approaches to surface the most common conversions while still supporting edge cases.

A highlight was running my first informal usability session — I showed some of the background remover prototypes to a few creators who use Playground regularly and got their unfiltered reactions. The feedback was eye-opening and led to some meaningful changes.
sections.deadlines:
The Alice landing page is in its final review stage — one more round of tweaks and it should be ready for development handoff next week.

Progress:

  • ✓ Alice landing page — final design iteration
  • ✓ Background remover — high-fidelity designs in progress
  • → Image converter — detailed flow mapping underway
  • ✓ First informal usability session completed

Next up: Finishing the background remover high-fidelity mockups, Alice handoff to engineering, and planning a more structured round of user testing for the creator tools.
sections.learning:
The biggest takeaway from this period was about the power of user feedback. I had assumptions about how the background remover should work, and watching actual creators interact with the prototype challenged some of those assumptions. For example, I assumed users would want granular control over the removal process, but most just wanted a one-click solution with minimal tweaking.

I also learned more about designing within constraints. Playground's design system is comprehensive, and while that's great for consistency, it sometimes means I can't do exactly what I want visually. Learning to work creatively within those boundaries has been a valuable exercise.

On a more practical level, I've been getting better at managing multiple design files and keeping my Figma workspace organized. Sounds mundane, but when you're working on three features simultaneously, good file hygiene saves a lot of time.
sections.environment:
The team rhythm is really clicking now. I know when people are available for quick feedback, when to schedule deeper reviews, and when to just put my head down and work. That kind of operational awareness only comes with time.

I've been having more one-on-one conversations with engineers about the features I'm designing, which has been invaluable. Understanding their perspective early — what's easy, what's hard, what's risky — helps me make better design decisions upfront rather than discovering problems during handoff.

Atlanta in spring is beautiful. I've been exploring different neighborhoods on weekends and found a great running trail near Piedmont Park. Having a life outside work makes the work better, I think.
sections.strengths:
Attention to detail in high-fidelity design work — the team has commented positively on the polish level of my mockups.

Willingness to seek out and act on user feedback, even when it challenges my initial assumptions.

Growing comfort with presenting work-in-progress to stakeholders without feeling like everything needs to be perfect.
sections.growth:
Structuring usability sessions more formally — my first one was useful but a bit unstructured. I want to get better at writing discussion guides and capturing findings systematically.

Managing scope — I sometimes go deeper on details than necessary at a given stage, spending time on pixel-perfect polish when the design direction itself still needs validation.
sections.growthPlan:
For usability testing, I've been reading about different research methods and plan to create a simple template for future sessions — goals, tasks, questions, and a consistent way to document findings.

For scope management, I'm trying to be more explicit about what stage a design is in (exploration, refinement, or final) and matching my level of detail to that stage.
sections.positive:
Hearing a creator say "oh, this is exactly what I needed" during the usability session was probably the highlight of my internship so far. It's easy to lose sight of real users when you're deep in pixels, so those moments of connection with the people you're designing for are incredibly motivating.

I also feel like I've hit a stride with the team. I'm no longer the new person figuring things out — I'm someone who contributes meaningfully to product discussions and whose design opinions are taken seriously. That's a great feeling.

title: From Ideas to Implementation
excerpt: Working on new feature concepts, designing the landing page for Alice, and transitioning from remote work to joining the team in Atlanta.

sections.activities:
Over the past two weeks the work has started becoming more concrete.

I've continued exploring potential creator tools, particularly features like background removal and image conversion. The focus has been translating these ideas into actual product flows and interface concepts. This involved wireframing different approaches and thinking through how these tools would fit into the existing Playground interface.

Another major task has been designing the landing page for our upcoming AI assistant called Alice. The goal is to communicate what Alice does in a clear and simple way while staying consistent with the Playground visual identity.

At the same time, I've been monitoring template usage and product metrics, looking at which templates are being used most and trying to understand patterns in how creators interact with the platform.

I've also been helping a bit with the operations side of the creator program, which gave me a better understanding of the relationship between the platform and its creator community.

A big personal milestone during this period was also arriving in Atlanta and starting to work with the team in person, which makes collaboration much easier.
sections.deadlines:
Some of the feature concepts are still being validated, but the direction is becoming clearer.

The Alice landing page is moving toward completion, and the next step will be refining the design and preparing it for development.

For the next few weeks, the focus will likely be on finalizing feature concepts and supporting developers during implementation.
sections.learning:
One interesting realization from these weeks was how important product data is when making design decisions.

Looking at template metrics gave useful context around what creators actually use on the platform. Sometimes the most valuable insights come from observing real behavior rather than purely designing new ideas.

Another big shift was working in person with the team after the first remote weeks. Being able to quickly discuss ideas and iterate together speeds up the design process significantly.
sections.environment:
The team environment is very open and discussion-driven.

Because Playground operates at a relatively large scale, many conversations revolve around how decisions will affect existing users, which adds an interesting layer to product design.

Being able to join leadership discussions and product conversations has been very valuable for understanding how different perspectives influence product decisions.
sections.strengths:
I feel comfortable jumping into product discussions and quickly translating ideas into visual concepts and flows that the team can react to.

My previous experience working with startups has also helped when it comes to adapting to fast-moving conversations and shifting priorities.
sections.growth:
One area I want to improve is structuring presentations of ideas more clearly, especially when several possible approaches exist.

Another is continuing to build a deeper understanding of the data and metrics side of product decisions, since those insights often guide which features should be prioritized.
sections.growthPlan:
To improve this, I've been spending more time reviewing product metrics and asking questions about how the team interprets the data.

I'm also trying to frame design ideas more clearly during discussions by explaining the problem, the reasoning behind the solution, and the expected impact.
sections.positive: These weeks felt like a transition from onboarding into actively contributing to product decisions. It's exciting to start seeing how early design ideas can evolve into features that might eventually be used by thousands of creators.

title: Starting at Playground
excerpt: First weeks getting familiar with the product, the team, and how new features move from idea to implementation at scale.

sections.activities:
The first couple of weeks were mainly about getting a deeper understanding of Playground as a product and how the team approaches building new features.

Since the internship focuses on designing and validating new product features, I started by getting involved in discussions around potential creator tools, including features like background removal and image conversion. My role has been helping explore how these tools could fit into the existing product experience. That involved conversations with the CEO and COO, thinking through possible implementations, and starting early wireframes.

Because Playground is used by a large number of creators every month, a big part of the work is making sure new features integrate naturally into the existing workflows rather than just adding more tools.

Alongside that, I started looking into template usage and creator activity, monitoring which templates are performing well and how creators interact with them. This helped give context for how the product is actually being used.

Most of my work so far has been around product thinking and early design exploration — mapping out flows, wireframing potential solutions, and discussing how ideas could realistically be implemented.
sections.deadlines:
The first weeks were more about exploration and alignment than strict deadlines.

Because new features need to work within an existing platform used by many people, the early stage is focused on making sure ideas make sense before committing development resources.

The goal over the next few weeks is to refine the concepts we've been exploring and move them toward clear prototypes and implementation plans.
sections.learning:
One thing that stood out quickly is how different it is designing inside an established product compared to building something from scratch.

At Playground, every feature needs to work within an existing design system, product logic, and technical constraints. Decisions also need to consider the impact on a large user base.

Having previous experience working with startups definitely helps here, because the discussions are often very product-oriented. The focus is less on isolated design decisions and more on how a feature affects the broader ecosystem of the platform.

It's also been interesting to see how quickly ideas move from casual discussions into something that might actually ship.
sections.environment:
The first part of the internship was remote, but I was still able to join product discussions and meetings with the team.

The environment is very collaborative and fairly direct. Ideas are discussed openly, and it's common to iterate quickly based on feedback.

I've also been able to join some leadership discussions, which gives a broader perspective on how the company thinks about product direction and growth.
sections.strengths:
One thing that has helped a lot so far is being comfortable working in early product discussions. Because I've previously worked with startup teams, I'm used to situations where ideas are still forming and need to be translated into something concrete.

I've also been able to move fairly quickly from discussions into wireframes and flows, which helps make abstract ideas easier for the team to evaluate.
sections.growth:
One area I want to improve is communicating design decisions more clearly in group discussions. Even when the ideas are solid, presenting them in a way that is concise and easy for everyone to evaluate is an important skill.

Another area is documenting flows and product decisions more clearly, especially since features eventually need to be implemented by developers.
sections.growthPlan:
To improve this, I've been making an effort to structure my thinking before presenting ideas, focusing more on the reasoning behind decisions rather than only the design outcome.

I've also started documenting flows and feature ideas more clearly so the team can reference them later during implementation.
sections.positive: These first weeks have been a strong introduction to the product and how the team works. I'm looking forward to moving from early exploration into more concrete prototypes and feature validation in the coming weeks.
